title: Data generation
text: can build single stochastic process following model constructors: wn() (white noise) ar1() (AR(1)) pl() (power-law) matern() (Matérn) rw() (random walk) flicker() (flicker / 1/f) constructor returns time_series_model object can plotted combined others using +.

title: Data generation
text: generated object generated_time_series numeric series $series:
code: model_single <- ar1(phi = 0.9, sigma2 = 1) series_single <- generate(model_single, n = 500) plot(series_single) head(series_single$series) ## [1] 0.3362423 1.6150311 1.1883829 1.6127387 1.0371249 0.4571655

title: Data generation
text: Use + build composite model multiple stochastic processes. result sum_model can passed generate().  composite output generated_composite_model_time_series : series (total sum) components (list component series) model (names component)
code: model_comp <- wn(sigma2 = 1) + ar1(phi = 0.8, sigma2 = 0.5) + pl(kappa = 0.3, sigma2 = 2) series_comp <- generate(model_comp, n = 500) plot(series_comp) names(series_comp) ## [1] "series"     "components" "n"          "model"      "parameters"

text: plot() method composite model stacks component vertically adds sum top. single models, plot() draws time series model name title.

title: Estimate a small network of GNSS station
code: # define some function for plotting ellipse <- function(hlaxa = 1, hlaxb = 1, theta = 0, xc = 0, yc = 0, newplot = F, npoints = 100, fill = F, fillColor = "black", ...) {   a <- seq(0, 2 * pi, length = npoints + 1)   x <- hlaxa * cos(a)   y <- hlaxb * sin(a)   alpha <- angle(x, y)   rad <- sqrt(x^2 + y^2)   xp <- rad * cos(alpha + theta) + xc   yp <- rad * sin(alpha + theta) + yc   if (newplot) {     plot(xp, yp, type = "l", ...)   } else {     lines(xp, yp, ...)     if (fill == T) {       polygon(xp, yp, border = F, col = fillColor)     }   }    invisible() }   angle <- function(x, y) {   angle2 <- function(xy) {     x <- xy[1]     y <- xy[2]     if (x > 0) {       atan(y / x)     } else {       if (x < 0 & y != 0) {         atan(y / x) + sign(y) * pi       } else {         if (x < 0 & y == 0) {           pi         } else {           if (y != 0) {             (sign(y) * pi) / 2           } else {             NA           }         }       }     }   }   apply(cbind(x, y), 1, angle2) }  # define function to make color transparent make_transparent <- function(colors, alpha = 0.5) {   # Ensure alpha is between 0 and 1   if (alpha < 0 || alpha > 1) {     stop("Alpha value must be between 0 and 1")   }    # Convert colors to RGB and add alpha   transparent_colors <- sapply(colors, function(col) {     rgb_val <- col2rgb(col) / 255     rgb(rgb_val[1], rgb_val[2], rgb_val[3], alpha = alpha)   })    return(transparent_colors) }

what: Estimate a small network in France
title: Estimate a small network of GNSS station
code: # Estimate little network in France all_station <- download_all_stations_ngl()  # download selected stations selected_station <- c("BSCN", "CERN", "SCDA", "GLRA", "STPS") df_network <- all_station %>% filter(station_name %in% selected_station) df_network  df_estimated_velocities <- data.frame(matrix(NA, nrow = dim(df_network)[1], ncol = 6)) for (station_index in seq_along(df_network$station_name)) {   station_name <- df_network$station_name[station_index]   # extract station   station_data <- download_station_ngl(station_name = station_name)   fit_N <- gmwmx2(station_data, n_seasonal = 2, component = "N", stochastic_model = "wn + pl")   fit_E <- gmwmx2(station_data, n_seasonal = 2, component = "E", stochastic_model = "wn + pl")   df_estimated_velocities[station_index, 1] <- station_name   df_estimated_velocities[station_index, 2:6] <- c(fit_N$beta_hat[2], fit_N$std_beta_hat[2], fit_E$beta_hat[2], fit_E$std_beta_hat[2], dim(fit_N$design_matrix_X)[1])    cat(paste0("Processing station ", station_name, " ", station_index, "/", length(df_network$station_name), "\n")) }  colnames(df_estimated_velocities) <- c("station_name", "estimated_trend_N", "std_estimated_trend_N", "estimated_trend_E", "std_estimated_trend_E", "time_series_length")  df_estimated_velocities$estimated_trend_N_scaled <- df_estimated_velocities$estimated_trend_N * 365.25 df_estimated_velocities$std_estimated_trend_N_scaled <- df_estimated_velocities$std_estimated_trend_N * 365.25 df_estimated_velocities$estimated_trend_E_scaled <- df_estimated_velocities$estimated_trend_E * 365.25 df_estimated_velocities$std_estimated_trend_E_scaled <- df_estimated_velocities$std_estimated_trend_E * 365.25  # transform longitude df_network$longitude2 <- ifelse(df_network$longitude < -180,   df_network$longitude + 360,   df_network$longitude )  # merge with location df_estimated_velocities_and_location <- dplyr::left_join(df_estimated_velocities, df_network, by = "station_name")  # print estimated North and East velocities head(df_estimated_velocities)  knitr::kable(df_estimated_velocities)

Define breaks for the color scale based on the raster values raster_values <- values(combined_raster) min_val <- min(raster_values, na.rm = TRUE) max_val <- max(raster_values, na.rm = TRUE)  # Generate breaks based on the range of the raster data num_colors <- length(custom_colors) breaks <- seq(min_val, max_val, length.out = num_colors + 1)   # plot plot(NA,   xlim = xlims, ylim = ylims, las = 1,   ylab = "", xlab = "",   xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n" )  axis(side = 1, at = seq(0, 12, by = 2), labels = (paste0(seq(0, 12, by = 2), " E"))) axis(side = 2, at = seq(40, 50, by = 2), labels = (paste0(seq(40, 50, by = 2), " N")), las = 1)  # Plot the elevation data raster::plot(combined_raster,   col = custom_colors,   # breaks=breaks,   add = TRUE, # Add to the existing plot   legend = FALSE ) # Disable default legend  # add axis for (i in seq(-150, 150, by = 2)) {   abline(v = i, col = "grey80", lty = 5) } for (i in seq(-90, 90, by = 2)) {   abline(h = i, col = "grey80", lty = 5) }  # add points for station data points(df_network$longitude2, df_network$latitude, pch = 16)   # set param for graph shift <- 0 scale_arrow <- 20 arrow_width <- .1 arrow_lwd <- 2 my_col <- c("#e96bff") scale_ellipses <- 3500 my_col_trans <- make_transparent(my_col, alpha = .3)  for (i in seq(nrow(df_estimated_velocities_and_location))) {   ellipse(     hlaxa = as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "std_estimated_trend_E_scaled"]) * scale_ellipses,     hlaxb = as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "std_estimated_trend_N_scaled"]) * scale_ellipses,     theta = 0,     xc = as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "longitude2"]) + as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "estimated_trend_E_scaled"]) * scale_arrow,     yc = as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "latitude"]) + as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "estimated_trend_N_scaled"]) * scale_arrow,     fill = T,     fillColor = my_col_trans[1],     lw = 1,     col = my_col_trans[1]   )    x0 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "longitude2"])   y0 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "latitude"])   x1 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "longitude2"] + df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "estimated_trend_E_scaled"] * scale_arrow)   y1 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "latitude"] + df_estimated_velocities_and_location[i, "estimated_trend_N_scaled"] * scale_arrow)    shape::Arrows(     x0 = x0, y0 = y0, x1 = x1, y1 = y1,     col = my_col,     arr.type = "triangle",     arr.length = .10,     arr.width = arrow_width,     lwd = arrow_lwd   ) }  # add text(   x = df_estimated_velocities_and_location$longitude2, y = df_estimated_velocities_and_location$latitude,   labels = df_estimated_velocities_and_location$station_name, pos = 3, cex = 0.8, col = "black" )  # define cities df_city <- tibble(address = c("Genève", "Clermont-Ferrand", "Dijon"))   # Load geolocalisation of cities df_geo <- df_city %>%   geocode_combine(     queries = list(       list(method = "osm")     ),     global_params = list(address = "address"),     cascade = FALSE   )  df_city_2 <- cbind(df_city, df_geo) df_city_2$City <- df_city_2$address  # Add city to map points(x = df_city_2$lon, y = df_city_2$lat, pch = 15, col = "black")  cex_size_city <- .7 for (i in seq(dim(df_city_2)[1])) {   text(     x = df_city_2$lon[i], y = df_city_2$lat[i],     labels = df_city_2$City[i],     adj = c(0, 2), col = "black",     cex = cex_size_city   ) }   # add a legend twenty_mm_per_year <- .02 twenty_mm_per_year_mm_per_year_scaled <- twenty_mm_per_year * scale_arrow x_start <- xlims[2] - 5 y <- ylims[1] + .3 segments(x0 = x_start, y0 = y, x1 = x_start + twenty_mm_per_year_mm_per_year_scaled, y1 = y) delta_y <- .1 segments(x0 = x_start, x1 = x_start, y0 = y + delta_y, y1 = y - delta_y) segments(x0 = x_start + twenty_mm_per_year_mm_per_year_scaled, x1 = x_start + twenty_mm_per_year_mm_per_year_scaled, y0 = y + delta_y, y1 = y - delta_y) txt_size <- .8 text(   x = x_start + twenty_mm_per_year_mm_per_year_scaled / 2,   y = y + .1,   pos = 3, cex = txt_size,   labels = "20 mm/year" )

title: Estimate a model
text: gmwmx2 R package allows estimate linear model correlated residuals presence missing data. precisely, assume following model: \[\begin{equation}     \boldsymbol{Y} = \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim \mathcal{F}\left\{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\left(\boldsymbol{\gamma}\right)\right\}, \end{equation}\] \(\boldsymbol{X} \\mathbb{R}^{n \times p}\) design matrix observed predictors, \(\boldsymbol{\beta} \\mathbb{R}^p\) regression parameter vector \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \{\varepsilon_{}\}_{=1,\ldots,n}\) zero-mean process following unspecified joint distribution \(\mathcal{F}\) positive-definite covariance function \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(\boldsymbol{\gamma}) \\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\) characterizing second-order dependence structure process parameterized vector \(\boldsymbol{\gamma} \\boldsymbol{\Gamma} \subset \mathbb{R}^q\). define random variable \(\boldsymbol{Z} =\{Z_{}\}_{=1,\ldots,n}\) describes missing observation mechanism. specifically, vector \(\boldsymbol{Z} \\{0, 1\}^n\) binary-valued stationary process independent \(\boldsymbol{Y}\) expectation \(\mu(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}) = \mathbb{E}[Z_i] \[0, \, 1)\), \(\forall \, \), covariance matrix \(\boldsymbol{\Lambda}(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}) = \mathbb{V}[\boldsymbol{Z}] \\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\) whose structure assumed known parameter vector \(\boldsymbol{\vartheta} \\boldsymbol{\Upsilon} \subset \mathbb{R}^k\) assume observe stochastic process \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{Y}} = \boldsymbol{Z} \odot \boldsymbol{Y}\), \(\odot\) denotes Hadamard product. Hence, \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{Y}} \\mathbb{R}^n = \boldsymbol{Y} \odot \boldsymbol{Z}\) represents observed process vector null elements positions observations missing. Using \(\otimes\) denote Kronecker product, define \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{X}} = \boldsymbol{Z} \otimes \boldsymbol{1}^T \odot \boldsymbol{X} \\mathbb{R}^{n \times p}\) design matrix \(\boldsymbol{X}\) zero-valued vectors rows observations missing \(\boldsymbol{Y}\) (\(\boldsymbol{1}\) represents vector ones dimension \(p\)). estimate parameters \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) least square estimator: \[\begin{equation}     \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \left(\tilde{\boldsymbol{X}}^T \tilde{\boldsymbol{X}}\right)^{-1} \tilde{\boldsymbol{X}}^T \tilde{\boldsymbol{Y}}. \end{equation}\] compute estimated residuals \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} = {\tilde{\boldsymbol{Y}}} - \tilde{{\boldsymbol{X}}} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\). estimate Maximum Likelihood Estimator parameters \(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}\) missingness process \(\boldsymbol{Z}\) assuming \(\boldsymbol{Z}\) generated Markov model following transition probabilities: \[\begin{equation}     \label{eq:markov_model_def}     \begin{aligned} & P\left( Z_2=1 \mid Z_1=1\right)=1 - p_1 \\ & P\left(Z_2=1 \mid Z_1=0\right) = p_2 \\ & P\left(Z_2=0 \mid Z_1=1\right)=p_1 \\ & P\left(Z_2=0 \mid Z_1=0\right)=1-p_2. \end{aligned} \end{equation}\] estimate parameters \(\boldsymbol{\gamma}\) using Generalized method Wavelet Moments approach (Guerrier et al. 2013) using fact variance-covariance matrix \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}\) given : \[\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}}(\boldsymbol{\gamma}) = [\boldsymbol{\Lambda}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}) + \mu(\hat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}})^2 \mathbf{1} \mathbf{1}^T ] \odot ( \boldsymbol{} - \boldsymbol{P})\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(\boldsymbol{\gamma}) (\boldsymbol{} - \boldsymbol{P})\] \(\boldsymbol{P} = \boldsymbol{X}(\boldsymbol{X}^T\boldsymbol{X})^{-1}\boldsymbol{X}^T\) \(\boldsymbol{}\) identity matrix dimension \(n\times n\). precisely, rely result Xu et al. (2017) provide computationally efficient form theoretical Allan variance (equivalent Haar wavelet variance constant) zero-mean stochastic processes \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\) avoid computing large matrices multiplication objective function. Indeed Xu et al. (2017) generalize results Zhang (2008) zero-mean non-stationary processes using averages diagonals super-diagonals covariance matrix \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\). implies GMWM, uses form, require storage \(n \times n\) covariance matrix \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\), vector dimension \(n\) plugged explicit formula consisting linear combination elements vector (elements averages diagonal super-diagonals covariance matrix).

text: estimate trajectory model (see e.g., Bevis Brown (2014) details), construct design matrix \(\boldsymbol{X}\) \(\)-th component vector \(\mathbf{X} {{\boldsymbol{\beta}}}\) can described follows \(t_i\) representing \(^{th}\) ordered time point (epoch) indicated Modified Julian Date \(t_0\) representing reference epoch located exactly middle start end point time series: \(\) initial position reference epoch \(t_0\), \(b\) velocity parameter, \(c_h\) \(d_h\) periodic motion parameters (\(h = 1\) \(h = 2\) represent annual semi-annual seasonal terms, respectively \(f_1 = \frac{1}{365.25}\) \(f_2 = \frac{2}{365.25}\)). offset terms models earthquakes, equipment changes human intervention \(e_j\) magnitude step epochs \(t_j\), \(r\) total number offsets, \(H\) Heaviside step function defined \(H(x):= \begin{cases}1, & x \geq 0 \\ 0, & x<0\end{cases}\) last term allow model post-seismic deformation (see e.g., Sobrero et al. title: Estimate a model
text: generally recognized noise GNSS time series best described combination colored noise plus white noise (et al. 2017; Langbein 2008; Williams et al. 2004; Bos et al. 2013) white noise generally model noise high frequencies colored noise model lower frequencies spectrum. large study noise properties GNSS signals, concluded optimal noise models 80–90% GNSS time series signals power law white noise model white noise flicker/pink noise model. package currently support stochastic model specification. precisely, power spectrum power-law noise following form: \[ P(f)=P_0\left(f / f_s\right)^\kappa \] \(f\) frequency, \(P_0\) constant, \(f_s\) sampling frequency exponent \(\kappa\) called spectral index. Many stochastic noise can expressed , example, spectral index \(\kappa=0\) produces white noise, spectral index \(\kappa=-2\) produces red noise random walk spectral index \(\kappa=-1\) produce flicker noise, also called pink noise. Granger (1980) Hosking (1981) showed power-law noise spectral index \(-1\) \(1\) can obtained using fractional differencing Gaussian noise: \[ (1-B)^{-\kappa / 2} \mathbf{v} \] \(B\) backward-shift operator \(\left(B v_i=v_{-1}\right)\) \(\mathbf{v}\) vector independent identically distributed (IID) Gaussian noise. Following Hosking’s definition fractional differencing, obtain \[ \begin{aligned} (1-B)^{-\kappa / 2} & =\sum_{=0}^{\infty}\binom{-\kappa / 2}{}(-B)^\\ & =1-\frac{\kappa}{2} B-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\kappa}{2}\left(1-\frac{\kappa}{2}\right) B^2+\ldots \\ & =\sum_{=0}^{\infty} h_i \end{aligned} \] coefficient \(h_i\) can computed using following recurrence relation (Kasdin Walter 1992): \[ \begin{aligned} h_0 & =1 \\ h_i & =\left(-\frac{\kappa}{2}-1\right) \frac{h_{-1}}{} \quad \text { } >0 \end{aligned} \] Assuming infinite sequence zero-mean white noise \(\mathbf{v}\), variance \(\sigma_{P L}^2\), spectral index \(kappa > -1\) autocovariance \(\gamma(\tau)=\operatorname{Cov}\left(X_t, X_{t+\tau}\right)=\mathbb{E}\left[\left(X_t-\mu\right)\left(X_{t+\tau}-\mu\right)\right]\) (Bos et al. 2008): \[ \begin{aligned} & \gamma(0)=\sigma_{p l}^2 \frac{\Gamma(1-\alpha)}{\left(\Gamma\left(1-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)\right)^2} \\ & \gamma(\tau)=\frac{\frac{\alpha}{2}+\tau-1}{-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha}+\tau} \gamma(\tau-1) \text { } \tau>0 \end{aligned} \] argument stochastic_model set "wn + pl", stochastic model considered includes white noise power-law specified stationary autocovariance structure. parameters estimated : \(\sigma^2_{W N}\), \(\kappa\) (constrained greater \(-1\)) \(\sigma^2_{P L}\). argument stochastic_model set "wn + fl", stochastic model considered includes white noise flicker noise (stationary power-law noise spectral index \(\kappa=-1\)) variance covariance flicker noise \(\omega\) obtained follows (see e.g., (Bos et al. 2008)): \[ \operatorname{Cov}(\omega) = \sigma^2_{F L}\mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{U} \] \[ \mathbf{U}=\left(\begin{array}{cccc} h_0 & h_1 & \ldots & h_n \\ 0 & h_0 & & h_{n-1} \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \ldots & h_0 \end{array}\right) \] coefficients \(h_i\) computed considering spectral index \(\kappa=-1\). stochastic parameters estimated : \(\sigma^2_{W N}\), \(\sigma^2_{F L}\) \(\kappa\) fixed \(-1\).

code: # plot all stations par(mar = c(2.5, 3.5, 1, 1))  # sett plot limits xlims <- c(-180, 180) ylims <- c(-90, 90)  # plot plot(NA,   xlim = xlims, ylim = ylims, las = 1,   ylab = "", xlab = "",   xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n" )  # Define longitude labels (X° E/W) lon_labels <- sapply(seq(-180, 180, by = 30), function(x) {   if (x < 0) {     paste0(abs(x), "° W")   } else if (x > 0) {     paste0(x, "° E")   } else {     "0°"   } })  # Define latitude labels (X° N/S) lat_labels <- sapply(seq(-90, 90, by = 20), function(y) {   if (y < 0) {     paste0(abs(y), "° S")   } else if (y > 0) {     paste0(y, "° N")   } else {     "0°"   } })  # Plot the axes axis(side = 1, at = seq(-180, 180, by = 30), labels = lon_labels) axis(side = 2, at = seq(-90, 90, by = 20), labels = lat_labels, las = 1)  plot(countries_sf$geometry, add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = 2)  # # add axis for (i in seq(-180, 180, by = 30)) {   abline(v = i, col = "grey80", lty = 5) } for (i in seq(-90, 90, by = 20)) {   abline(h = i, col = "grey80", lty = 5) }  my_col <- c("#e96bff") my_col_trans <- make_transparent(my_col, alpha = .4)  # set param for graph shift <- 0 scale_arrow <- 200 arrow_width <- .1 arrow_lwd <- 2  for (i in seq(nrow(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx))) {   x0 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "longitude"])   y0 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "latitude"])   x1 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "longitude"] + df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "estimated_trend_E_scaled"] * scale_arrow)   y1 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "latitude"] + df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "estimated_trend_N_scaled"] * scale_arrow)    shape::Arrows(     x0 = x0, y0 = y0, x1 = x1, y1 = y1,     col = my_col_trans,     arr.type = "triangle",     arr.length = .10,     arr.width = arrow_width,     lwd = arrow_lwd   ) }

title: Plot a large network of GNSS data
code: # Zoom in the US # set plot limits xlims <- c(-130, -60) ylims <- c(30, 55)   # plot plot(NA,   xlim = xlims, ylim = ylims, las = 1,   ylab = "", xlab = "",   xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n" )  # Define longitude labels (X° E/W) lon_labels <- sapply(seq(-180, 180, by = 10), function(x) {   if (x < 0) {     paste0(abs(x), "° W")   } else if (x > 0) {     paste0(x, "° E")   } else {     "0°"   } })  # Define latitude labels (X° N/S) lat_labels <- sapply(seq(-90, 90, by = 10), function(y) {   if (y < 0) {     paste0(abs(y), "° S")   } else if (y > 0) {     paste0(y, "° N")   } else {     "0°"   } })  # Plot the axes axis(side = 1, at = seq(-180, 180, by = 10), labels = lon_labels) axis(side = 2, at = seq(-90, 90, by = 10), labels = lat_labels, las = 1)   plot(countries_sf$geometry, add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = 2)  # # add axis for (i in seq(-180, 180, by = 10)) {   abline(v = i, col = "grey80", lty = 5) } for (i in seq(-90, 90, by = 10)) {   abline(h = i, col = "grey80", lty = 5) }  my_col <- c("#e96bff") my_col_trans <- make_transparent(my_col, alpha = .4)  # set param for graph shift <- 0 scale_arrow <- 200 arrow_width <- .1 arrow_lwd <- 2  for (i in seq(nrow(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx))) {   x0 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "longitude"])   y0 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "latitude"])   x1 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "longitude"] + df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "estimated_trend_E_scaled"] * scale_arrow)   y1 <- as.numeric(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "latitude"] + df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx[i, "estimated_trend_N_scaled"] * scale_arrow)    shape::Arrows(     x0 = x0, y0 = y0, x1 = x1, y1 = y1,     col = my_col_trans,     arr.type = "triangle",     arr.length = .10,     arr.width = arrow_width,     lwd = arrow_lwd   ) }  my_col_trans2 <- make_transparent(my_col, alpha = .6) points(df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx$longitude, df_estimated_velocities_gmwmx$latitude, pch = 16, col = my_col_trans2)

title: AR(1) process — ar1
text: Constructs time_series_model stationary AR(1) process parameter phi innovation variance sigma2. model \(X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \; \varepsilon_t \stackrel{\text{..d.}}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)\). autocovariance \( \gamma(h) = \mathrm{cov}(X_t, X_{t+h}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{1 - \phi^2}\,\phi^{\lvert h \rvert} \).

title: Flicker noise process — flicker
text: Constructs time_series_model flicker noise variance sigma2. process spectral density \(S(f) \propto \frac{1}{|f|}\). Hence, \(\kappa = -1\) (Bos et al., 2008). process non-stationary covariance matrix assumed given $$ \mathbf C = \sigma^2 \mathbf U^\top \mathbf U, $$ \(\mathbf U \\mathbb{R}^{N \times N}\) upper-triangular Toeplitz matrix entries $$ U_{,j} = \begin{cases} h_{j-}, & j \ge , \\ 0,       & j < , \end{cases} \qquad ,j = 1, \ldots, N. $$ coefficients \(\{h_i\}_{\ge 0}\) define causal linear filter given recursively $$ h_0 = 1, \qquad h_i = \left(- \frac{\kappa}{2} - 1\right)\frac{h_{-1}}{}, \quad > 0. $$

title: Stationary Power-Law process — pl
text: Constructs time_series_model representing stationary power-law process parameters kappa sigma2. frequency domain, power-law process often described spectrum \(P(f) = P_0 f^{\kappa}\) (Bos et al., 2008), \(f\) frequency, \(P_0\) constant \(\kappa\) spectral index. Note use convention power spectral density satisfies \(P(f) \propto |f|^{\kappa}\), \(\kappa > -1\) ensures second-order stationarity. corresponds alternative notation \(P(f) \propto |f|^{-\alpha}\) \(\alpha = -\kappa\). autocovariance used (Hosking, 1981) \(\gamma(0) = \sigma^{2} \frac{\Gamma(1+\kappa)}{\Gamma\left(1+\kappa/2\right)^2}\), \(h > 0\) \(\gamma(h) =\mathrm{cov}(X_t, X_{t+h}) = \frac{-\kappa/2 + h - 1}{\kappa/2 + h}\,\gamma(h-1)\).

code: pl(kappa = NULL, sigma2 = NULL)

title: Stationary Power-Law process — pl

text: time_series_model object.

title: Stationary Power-Law process — pl
